questions.text: Everything available in our environment which can be used to satisfy our needs, provided it is technologically accessible, economically feasible and culturally acceptable is called:
questions.explanation: This is the comprehensive geographical definition of a resource.

questions.text: On the basis of origin, resources are classified as:
questions.explanation: Biotic resources are obtained from the biosphere (life), while abiotic resources are composed of non-living things.

questions.text: Rocks and metals are examples of:
questions.explanation: Since they are non-living, they are classified as abiotic.

questions.text: Solar and wind energy, water, forests and wildlife are examples of:
questions.explanation: These resources can be renewed or reproduced by physical, chemical or mechanical processes.

questions.text: The first International Earth Summit was held in June 1992 in:
questions.explanation: More than 100 heads of states met in Brazil for the United Nations Conference on Environment and Development (UNCED).

questions.text: What was the main purpose of Agenda 21 adopted at the 1992 Earth Summit?
questions.explanation: It is a declaration to combat environmental damage, poverty, and disease through global cooperation.

questions.text: Resources which are found in a region but have not been utilized are called:
questions.explanation: For example, Rajasthan and Gujarat have enormous potential for solar and wind energy but they haven't been fully developed.

questions.text: Which state in India is very rich in minerals and coal deposits but lacks in infrastructural development?
questions.explanation: Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh are rich in mineral resources.

questions.text: The subset of 'Stock', which can be put into use with existing technology but their use has not been started is called:
questions.explanation: Reserves are kept for meeting future requirements.

questions.text: The total geographical area of India is:
questions.explanation: India occupies 2.4% of the total world area with 3.28 million sq km.

questions.text: About ________ percent of the land area in India is plain, which provides facilities for agriculture and industry.
questions.explanation: India has 43% plains, 30% mountains, and 27% plateaus.

questions.text: Which soil is most widely spread and important soil of India, covering the entire northern plains?
questions.explanation: Alluvial soil is deposited by the three important Himalayan river systems – the Indus, the Ganga and the Brahmaputra.

questions.text: Old alluvial soil is known as ________ and new alluvial soil is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Bangar has more kankar nodules and is less fertile than Khadar.

questions.text: Black soil is also known as ________ soil.
questions.explanation: Regur comes from the Telugu word 'Reguda' meaning black cotton soil.

questions.text: Which soil is ideal for growing cotton?
questions.explanation: Black soil is typical of the Deccan trap region and is very fine and clayey.

questions.text: Laterite has been derived from the Latin word 'later' which means:
questions.explanation: Laterite soil develops under tropical and subtropical climates with alternate wet and dry seasons.

questions.text: Which soil is very useful for growing tea and coffee?
questions.explanation: Laterite soil in hilly areas of Karnataka, Kerala and Tamil Nadu is very useful for these crops.

questions.text: Red laterite soils in Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh and Kerala are more suitable for crops like:
questions.explanation: Specific soil treatments make laterite suitable for cashew nuts.

questions.text: When the topsoil is washed away by water running over large areas, it is called:
questions.explanation: Sheet erosion occurs on gentle slopes when the vegetative cover is thin.

questions.text: The running water cuts through the clayey soils and makes deep channels called:
questions.explanation: Gully erosion makes the land unfit for cultivation, often seen in the Chambal basin.

questions.text: Which method of soil conservation involves planting lines of trees to create shelter?
questions.explanation: Shelter belts have contributed significantly to the stabilization of sand dunes in Western India.

questions.text: Ploughing along the contour lines to decelerate the flow of water down the slopes is called:
questions.explanation: This is a key method for soil conservation in hilly regions.

questions.text: Land left without cultivation for one or less than one agricultural year is called:
questions.explanation: Fallow land is left uncultivated to allow the soil to regain its fertility naturally.

questions.text: Which state has the maximum area under the Net Sown Area in India?
questions.explanation: Punjab and Haryana have over 80% of their total area as net sown area.

questions.text: In which state is mining the main cause of land degradation?
questions.explanation: Mining sites are abandoned after excavation work is complete leaving deep scars.

questions.text: In states like Punjab, Haryana and western Uttar Pradesh, the main cause of land degradation is:
questions.explanation: Over irrigation leads to waterlogging which increases salinity and alkalinity in the soil.

questions.text: Resources which are surveyed and their quality and quantity have been determined for utilization are:
questions.explanation: The development of resources depends on technology and level of their feasibility.

questions.text: Individual, Community, National and International are categories based on:
questions.explanation: This classification focuses on who has the right to use the resource.

questions.text: Territorial water of a country extends up to how many nautical miles from the coast?
questions.explanation: 12 nautical miles (about 22.2 km) is the limit of national oceanic territory.

questions.text: The Oceanic resources beyond 200 nautical miles of the Exclusive Economic Zone belong to:
questions.explanation: No individual country can utilize these without the concurrence of international institutions.

questions.text: What is the desired percentage of forest area in the country as outlined in the National Forest Policy (1952)?
questions.explanation: 33% is considered essential for the maintenance of the ecological balance.

questions.text: Land which is left uncultivated for more than 5 agricultural years is:
questions.explanation: This land can be brought under cultivation after some improvement measures.

questions.text: Black soils are generally poor in ________ content.
questions.explanation: They are rich in nutrients like lime and potash but lack phosphorous.

questions.text: The lower horizons of the Arid soil are occupied by Kankar because of the increasing ________ content downwards.
questions.explanation: The Kankar layer formation restricts the infiltration of water.

questions.text: Large fields divided into strips and leaving strips of grass to grow between crops is called:
questions.explanation: This breaks up the force of the wind, preventing soil erosion.

questions.text: Sustainable development means:
options: Development should take place without damaging the environment
questions.explanation: It emphasizes that development in the present should not compromise the needs of future generations.

questions.text: Which book introduced the concept of Resource Conservation for the first time in 1974?
questions.explanation: Written by Schumacher, it presented Gandhian philosophy on conservation.

questions.text: The Brundtland Commission Report (1987) introduced the concept of:
questions.explanation: This report was later published as a book entitled 'Our Common Future'.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a human-made resource?
questions.explanation: Forests are natural resources, while the others are created by humans using technology.

questions.text: The main aim of resource planning is:
options: To use resources as quickly as possible
To identify and use resources judiciously
questions.explanation: Resource planning is essential for sustainable existence of all forms of life.
